Final Project

Paloma Mitra

May 12th, 2021 (Final Draft)

Question of interest

What is the relationship between types of incentives received and how community health workers (Kenya and Uganda) score various job attributes?

Data & Introduction

Introduction: The data used in this project comes from surveys administered in Kenya and Uganda with community health workers, gathering information on the types and amount of various incentives (ex. trainings, compensation) they currently receive, respondent demographics, and how important various job attributes were to them. Part of this survey was also a discrete choice experiment meant to elucidate how important job attributes were relative to one another, but this was not analyzed in this project.

The data is not downloadable for privacy reasons, but survey tools have been uploaded to GitHub for reference.

Variables of interest

In this project, variables about existing job attributes/incentives (ex. compensation, training), respondent demographics, and six job attributes pulled from a motivation module in the survey.

  • Outcome variable: 6 separate job attributes: quality of supervision, feelings of security, growth opportunities, adaptive management, peer respect, and compensation.
  • Primary predictor variable: compensation: amount of monetary compensation currently received
  • Possible confounders: age, sex, religion, education, marital status, country, trainings
  • Potential effect modifiers: age, sex, religion, education, marital status, country

Directed acyclic graph (DAG)

Six separate regression models are created, but this is one generic DAG as the same underlying relationship was investigated for each outcome in the Motivation Module.

How variables were selected:

Primary Predictor(s)

Trainings: Across both countries, nearly all CHWs received all initial, practical, and refresher training.

Trainings
Characteristic Overall, N = 6101 Kenya, N = 2111 Uganda, N = 3991
Initial Training 600 (98%) 207 (98%) 393 (98%)
Practical Training 552 (90%) 184 (87%) 368 (92%)
Refresher Training 593 (97%) 202 (96%) 391 (98%)
All Trainings
All trainings received 531 (87%) 171 (81%) 360 (90%)
Not all trainings 79 (13%) 40 (19%) 39 (9.8%)

1 n (%)

Compensation: As trainings was not a very informative primary predictor because of the low variability in responses, I decided to choose compensation. However, as Uganda and Kenya have different currencies, this variable was standardized by converting to USD. Additionally, the initial boxplots for Ugandan compensation were fairly skewed, making a log10 conversion ideal for comparison within country type and across countries.

educate was a variable of interest before Dr. Jager’s comments. After combining ‘Never attended’, ‘Nursery’, and ‘Primary’ levels however, it did not remain a statistically significant variable in the regression model. When considering compensation amounts by education status, split by country, it is evident that the major difference is by country, where Kenyan CHWs receive higher amounts of monetary compensation across all education levels compared to Ugandan CHWs. While the upper and lower quartiles for Primary, Secondary, and College/University/Vocational in overlap, the upper quartile for Secondary and College/University/Vocational in Kenya exceeds the upper quartile for Primary. This indicates that education status within Uganda has less of an influence on amount of compensation received compared to in Kenya.

Outcomes

Analysis plan

Potential confounders will be included in the analysis.

As outcomes will be treated as binary, logistic regression will be used for each of the six models. To control for confounding, potential confounders were included in each model and were kept if results were statistically significant.

Combining Levels: The outcomes in this project are from a six question motivation module found in both surveys. 6 separate logistic regression models were created to address each question. In addition, the levels of the variable “Not Important” and “Important” were combined to form a level called “Less Important” so that logistic regression could be used (0, 1). Similarly, for some of the variables included in the Respondent Demographics table, levels with low numbers of respondents (<8-10%, with the exception of education) have been combined to form a level called “Other”.

By Outcome Discussion: This graph had more importance before combining levels, as now across educate levels, the percentage of those choosing ‘Very Important’ is similar across all education levels for all six outcomes as well as between outcomes.

###I also left this because I worked so hard on it >:(

Modeling

The results below are of the primary models constructed, and the second model selected based on what variables were statistically significant or necessary (primary predictor always included). AICs for the models were also compared and the model with the lower AIC was selected overall.

###I could not get the in-line code with gt and gtsummary to work at all, so I did it manually!

Quality of supervision

country: Holding all else constant, those from Uganda have a 128% increased odds of choosing ‘Very Important’ for how important quality of supervision is as a job attribute.

Quality of supervision: Model 1 \[log(Quality \ of \ supervision) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 \cdot (log(compensation)) + \beta_2 \cdot country + \beta_3 \cdot religion + \beta_4 \cdot age + \beta_5 \cdot marital + \beta_6 \cdot educate + \beta_7 \cdot sex\] Quality of supervision: Model 2 \[log(Quality \ of \ supervision) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 \cdot (log(compensation)) + \beta_2 \cdot country\]

Quality of supervision: Logistic Regression Model
Characteristic OR1 95% CI1 p-value
log10(compensation) 0.90 0.52, 1.54 0.7
Country
Kenya — —
Uganda 2.28 1.36, 3.82 0.002

1 OR = Odds Ratio, CI = Confidence Interval

Table 1: AIC Results
Model df AIC
Model 1 9 563.29
Model 2 3 557.52

Feeling secure

log10_compensation: Holding all else constant, a one-unit increase in log10(compensation) was associated with a 88% increased odds of choosing ‘Very Important’ for how important feeling secure is as a job attribute.

country: Holding all else constant, those from Uganda have a 200% increased odds of choosing ‘Very Important’ for how important feeling secure is as a job attribute.

Feeling secure: Model 1 \[log(Feeling \ secure) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 \cdot (log(compensation)) + \beta_2 \cdot country + \beta_3 \cdot religion + \beta_4 \cdot age + \beta_5 \cdot marital + \beta_6 \cdot educate + \beta_7 \cdot sex\] Feeling secure: Model 2 \[log(Feeling \ secure) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 \cdot (log(compensation)) + \beta_2 \cdot country\]

log10(compensation) 1.88 1.04, 3.45 0.039

Kenya — —
Uganda 3.00 1.73, 5.20 <0.001

Growth opportunities

Adaptive management

trainings: Holding all else constant, those who did not receive all requisite trainings have a 57% decreased odds of choosing ‘Very Important’ for how important adaptive management is as a job attribute.

Adaptive management: Model 1 \[log(adaptive \ management) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 \cdot (log(compensation)) + \beta_2 \cdot trainings + \beta_3 \cdot country + \beta_4 \cdot religion + \beta_5 \cdot age + \beta_6 \cdot marital + \beta_7 \cdot educate + \beta_8 \cdot sex\] Adaptive management: Model 2 \[log(adaptive \ management) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 \cdot (log(compensation)) + \beta_2 \cdot trainings\]

Peer respect

country: Holding all else constant, those from Uganda have a 112% increased odds of choosing ‘Very Important’ for how important peer respect is as a job attribute.

age: Holding all else constant, a one-unit increase in age was associated with a 3% increased odds of choosing ‘Very Important’ for how important peer respect is as a job attribute.

Peer Respect: Model 1 \[log(peer \ respect) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 \cdot (log(compensation)) + \beta_2 \cdot country + \beta_3 \cdot religion + \beta_4 \cdot age + \beta_5 \cdot marital + \beta_6 \cdot educate + \beta_7 \cdot sex\] Peer Respect: Model 2 \[log(peer \ respect) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 \cdot (log(compensation)) + \beta_2 \cdot country + \beta_3 \cdot age\]

Compensation

log10_compensation: Holding all else constant, a one-unit increase in log10(compensation) was associated with a 49% decreased odds of choosing ‘Very Important’ for how important compensation is as a job attribute.

country: Holding all else constant, those from Uganda have a 49% decreased odds of choosing ‘Very Important’ for how important compensation is as a job attribute.

Compensation: Model 1 \[log(Compensation) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 \cdot (log(compensation)) + \beta_2 \cdot country + \beta_3 \cdot religion + \beta_4 \cdot age + \beta_5 \cdot marital + \beta_6 \cdot educate + \beta_7 \cdot sex\] Compensation: Model 2 \[log(Compensation) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 \cdot (log(compensation)) + \beta_2 \cdot country\]

log10(compensation) 0.51 0.27, 0.96 0.038

Uganda 0.51 0.27, 0.93 0.034

1 OR = Odds Ratio, CI = Confidence Interval

Table 6: AIC Results
Model df AIC
Model 1 9 534.08
Model 2 3 526.86

Comparing Overall Results: Overall, country level held the largest bearing on whether or not a CHW was likely to choose ‘Very Important’ for any select job attribute. While individual attributes–peer respect and adaptive management for instance–had, individually statistically significant variables such as age and trainings, trends across the six job attribute outcomes were less clear for those variables.

For the primary predictor, compensation, increasing log10(compensation) did confer a lower “risk” of choosing ‘Very Important’ for the outcome job attribute compensation. For feeling secure there were increased odds for increasing log10(compensation). This may reflect on some mindset shift that comes with financial stability, but the influence of country is likely important when further exploring this relationship as Kenyan CHWs are also paid higher on average.
